planting.note: Divide suckers in spring or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun after frost danger has passed; rice paper plant spreads by underground stolons and requires ample space — allow 6–8 feet on all sides. Cut to ground after frost in zone 8; it rebounds vigorously from the root.

directions: Divide underground stolons or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; rice paper plant (Chinese rice paper plant) is a bold, dramatic Asian shrub that produces some of the largest leaves of any hardy to zone 8 plant — enormous, deeply lobed, palmate leaves that can reach 3 feet or more across on stems that rise to 6–15 feet, creating an instantly tropical, jungle-like effect with no equivalent in temperate gardening. The felted undersides of the leaves provide a contrasting silver-gray texture. The traditional Chinese paper used for making rice paper paintings is made from the pith of the stems. It spreads steadily by underground stolons to form expanding colonies, which can be managed by mowing around the perimeter; this spreading habit also makes it excellent for covering large areas. In zone 8, top growth is killed by frost but the plant rebounds from underground stolons vigorously each spring, often reaching 8–10 feet by summer. 'Steroidal Giant' is a selection with even larger leaves.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; rohdea tolerates the deepest, driest shade of any ornamental perennial for zone 7–10 and is specifically useful in the most difficult dark corners of the landscape.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; rohdea (sacred lily, Nippon lily, omoto) is a Japanese native that has been cultivated and revered in Japan for centuries as a symbol of good fortune and longevity, with thousands of cultivars developed by Japanese enthusiasts — some producing extravagantly variegated, ruffled, crested, or otherwise unusual foliage prized as collector's items. For garden use, the plain species produces handsome, glossy, dark-green, strap-shaped leaves in dense, upright clumps and tolerates extreme shade conditions — including the dark, dry shade under mature trees — that defeat most other evergreen perennials. Inconspicuous spike-like flowers in late spring are followed by brilliant red berries in autumn. Extremely long-lived, drought-tolerant once established, and essentially maintenance-free. An excellent deer-resistant shade plant for zone 7–10 gardens. Berries are toxic.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide rhizomes or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade in spring; butterfly iris spreads by stolons to form large colonies and is excellent as a groundcover in shaded spots where grasses will not grow.

directions: Divide rhizomes in spring or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade; butterfly iris (Japanese iris, fan iris) is a shade-tolerant, spreading Japanese iris that forms broad, fan-shaped clumps of flat, evergreen, sword-like leaves and produces a profusion of delicate, orchid-like, lavender-blue flowers with distinctive ruffled, fringed petals marked with yellow and white — the frilled, butterfly wing-like petals give the flower an unusually refined, delicate quality that differs from the more boldly structured bearded and Louisiana irises. It is one of the most shade-tolerant of all irises, thriving in partial shade under high trees where bearded irises would fail to bloom. The spreading, stoloniferous rhizomes create dense, weed-suppressing groundcover colonies in moist, partially shaded spots. 'Ledger's Variety' and 'Uwaduraku' are notable cultivars. Excellent for naturalized woodland edge plantings.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; Fargesia bamboos are clump-forming (not running/invasive) and can be planted without containment barriers, unlike Phyllostachys and other runner bamboos.

directions: Divide established clumps or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; umbrella bamboo (Chinese fountain bamboo) is one of the most ornamental and garden-appropriate of all bamboos for temperate gardens — a graceful, arching, clump-forming (non-invasive) bamboo that produces dense clusters of slender, elegant culms (canes) that arch gracefully outward and downward like a fountain of cascading foliage, bearing short, narrow, lance-shaped leaves that create a rustling, whispering sound in the breeze. Unlike the running bamboos (Phyllostachys, Pseudosasa) that spread invasively by underground rhizomes, Fargesia bamboos form compact, tightly restrained clumps that expand slowly and require no containment barriers. Tolerates shade better than most bamboos. Excellent as a screen, specimen, or large container plant. Unlike some Fargesias, this species flowers infrequently. 'Simba' and 'Jumbo' are popular cultivar selections.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide rhizomatous colonies or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; Japanese ardisia spreads slowly by underground rhizomes to form a dense, weed-suppressing, evergreen carpet and is an excellent low-maintenance groundcover for problem shade areas.

directions: Divide rhizomatous colonies or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Japanese ardisia (marlberry, senryo-alternative) is a low-growing, spreading, evergreen Asian shrub that forms a dense, weed-suppressing groundcover in shaded conditions — the toothed, glossy, oval leaves in whorled clusters give the plant a neat, refined appearance throughout the year. Small, white, nodding flowers in summer are followed by clusters of brilliant red berries that persist through winter and into the following spring, providing attractive color at a season when little else is colorful in the shade garden. Plants spread by underground stolons to gradually colonize shaded areas, creating dense, uniform coverage under trees and in woodland settings. Much more cold-hardy than the invasive coral ardisia (A. crenata) and native to similar garden conditions. 'Hakutsuru' (white berries), 'Chirimen' (crinkled leaves), and 'Nishiki' (variegated) are cultivar selections.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
